4: The divine speech from the whirlwind (Job 38-41) is the OT's most extended meditation on the incomprehensibility and majesty of God. YHWH's questions ('Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding') do not answer Job's question about suffering — they reframe it by confronting Job with the Creator's perspective. The response to theodicy is not a logical explanation but a theophanic encounter: when Job sees YHWH, his questions are transformed, not answered (42:5: 'my eye sees you'; he is satisfied). This pattern — suffering resolved not by explanation but by encounter with God — points to the incarnation: the answer to the problem of suffering is not a theodicy argument but the Son of God entering the suffering and going through it.
1: wayyaʿan YHWH ʾet-ʾîyôḇ min-haśśᵉʿārāh wayyōʾmar: 'Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind and said.' The divine name YHWH appears here for the first time in the entire dialogue (chs. 3-37), where only ʾĕlôah (God) and šaddāy (Almighty) have been used. The shift to the covenant name is significant: Job is addressed not by the remote transcendent deity but by YHWH, the God of the covenant and of personal relationship. Śᵉʿārāh (whirlwind, storm-wind — a theophanic term): the whirlwind is YHWH's vehicle of revelation (cf. Ezek 1:4; Zech 9:14; Nah 1:3), consistent with the storm theophany tradition of Sinai (Exod 19:16-19) and the Psalms (Ps 18:10-15).
2: mî zeh maḥšîḵ ʿēṣāh bimillîm bᵉlî-ḏāʿat: 'Who is this who darkens counsel with words without knowledge?' The opening charge is forensic and rhetorical: maḥšîḵ ʿēṣāh (darkening counsel) — clouding what is clear, making wisdom unclear. Job's speeches, for all their emotional authenticity, have treated the divine governance as something that can be adjudicated by human speech (millîm) that lacks knowledge (bᵉlî ḏāʿat). The charge is not that Job lied but that he spoke beyond his competence. The same charge could be laid at the friends; YHWH addresses it to Job because Job is the one demanding an answer.
3: ḥăḡōr-nā kᵉḡeḇer ḥălāṣêḵā wᵉʾešʾālᵉḵā wᵉhôḏîʿēnî: 'Brace yourself like a man; I will question you and you must answer me.' ḥăḡōr kᵉḡeḇer (gird your loins like a man, a warrior) — the command to prepare for combat, addressed to warriors before battle (1 Kgs 20:11; Jer 1:17). God does not destroy Job with his power but invites him into a contest of questions. The role reversal is complete: Job has been demanding a hearing before God; now God gives it — but as questioner, not defendant. Wᵉhôḏîʿēnî (and you must instruct me/make me know) — the ultimate ironic impossible demand: the creature instructing the Creator.
5: mî-śām mᵉmaddêhā kî ṯēḏāʿ ʾô mî-nāṭāh ʿālêhā qāw: 'Who determined its measurements — do you know? Or who stretched the measuring line across it?' The question form (mî = who?) dominates the divine speech: 77 questions in chs. 38-41. Each question is a demonstration of divine knowledge that Job lacks and cannot claim. Qāw (measuring line, plumb line) — the architectural surveying tool used for the earth's dimensions. The earth was laid out with divine precision; Job was not present for the measurements and cannot claim knowledge of them.
6: ʿal-mah ʾadānêhā huṭbāʿû ʾô mî-yārāh ʾeḇen pinnātāh: 'On what were its foundations (ʾadānîm) set? Or who laid its cornerstone?' ʾAḏen (base, socket, foundation — also used for the tabernacle's silver bases, Exod 26:19) — the foundations of the earth are its divine-architected supports. ʾEḇen pinnāh (cornerstone) — the capstone that locks an arch or the first stone that determines alignment. The building-metaphor for creation (foundations, measuring line, cornerstone) will be used in the NT for Christ as the cornerstone (Ps 118:22; Matt 21:42; Eph 2:20) — the divine builder of creation becomes the cornerstone of the new creation.
7: bᵉron yaḥad koḵᵉḇê bōqer wayyārîʿû kol-bᵉnê ʾĕlōhîm: 'When the morning stars rang out together and all the divine beings shouted for joy?' Koḵᵉḇê bōqer (morning stars) and bᵉnê ʾĕlōhîm (sons of God, divine beings — the same term as 1:6; 2:1) — the angelic assembly that witnessed and celebrated creation. The cosmic joy at creation's completion (rûaʿ = shout with a ringing cry, used of battle-cries and worship, Ps 47:1; 98:4) is the inverse of the lament that pervades chs. 3-37: before there was suffering, there was rejoicing at the world's making. The question implies: were you there for that joy, Job?
8: wayyāsōḵ baḏᵉlāṯayim yām bᵉgîḥô mēreḥem yēṣēʾ: 'Who shut in the sea with doors when it burst out from the womb?' The sea's origin: gîḥô mēreḥem yēṣēʾ (when it burst out from the womb) — the sea is born like a creature, emerging from a womb. But God immediately confines it with doors (dalatāyim = double doors). The contrast between the sea's violent emergence (it bursts) and God's calm architectural response (doors and bars, v.10) is the creation theology of Gen 1:9-10 dramatized: chaos is bounded by divine decree. The sea as embodiment of chaos (cf. Ps 93:3-4; Isa 27:1; Rev 21:1) is controlled from birth.
9: bᵉśûmî ʿānān lᵉḇūšô waʿărāp̄el ḥᵉtullāṯô: 'When I made clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling cloth.' The extended birth metaphor continues: the newborn sea is clothed (lᵉḇûš = garment) in clouds and wrapped (ḥᵉtullāh = swaddling bands, the cloth used for newborns, Ezek 16:4; Luke 2:7) in ʿărāp̄el (thick darkness, the darkness of a storm or the Sinai theophany, Exod 20:21; Deut 4:11). God tends to the newborn sea as a parent, providing it coverings — but those coverings are also restraints. Divine care and divine boundary-setting are the same act.
10: wāʾešbōr ʿālāyw ḥuqqî wāʾāśîm bᵉrîaḥ ûḏᵉlāṯāyim: 'When I fixed its boundaries and set its bars and doors.' Ḥōq (decree, boundary, prescribed limit) — the same word used for the Torah's commandments; the sea has its own divinely given ḥōq: a decree about how far it may go (cf. Prov 8:29: 'he prescribed a limit for the sea'). Bᵉrîaḥ ûḏᵉlāṯāyim (bar and double doors) — the cosmic lockwork that holds the sea in its place. YHWH is the locksmith of the ocean; the forces of chaos are not suppressed by their own weakness but contained by divine architecture.
11: wāʾōmar ʿaḏ-pōh tāḇôʾ wᵉlōʾ tôsîp̄ wᵉp̄ō-yāśît bᵉgaʾôn gallêḵā: 'When I said, “This far you may come and no farther; here your proud waves will stop.”' The divine boundary-decree is given in direct speech — the most direct form of authority. ʿAḏ-pō tāḇôʾ wᵉlōʾ tôsîp̄: 'until here you will come and not add/continue' — the sea's advance is terminated by divine word. Bᵉgaʾôn gallêḵā: 'here the pride of your waves will be set/stopped' — the gaʾôn (majesty, pride) of the waves is the sea's power claiming space; God stops that claim with a word. The same divine word that stopped the primordial sea speaks to Job from the whirlwind.
12: hᵉmiyyāmêḵā ṣiwwîṯā bōqer yiddaʿᵉtāh šaḥar mᵉqōmô: 'Have you ever commanded the morning since your life began, or told the dawn where to take its place?' The dawn is a standing appointment (mᵉqôm = place, station) that it has kept since creation. Ṣiwwāh bōqer (commanded the morning) — the morning receives a daily divine command to rise. Šaḥar (dawn, early light) — the dawn knows its place and goes there; Job does not command it. The daily recurrence of dawn is a standing demonstration of divine faithfulness and sovereignty: creation's order is not automatic but God's ongoing governance.
13: lᵉʾĕḥōz biḵanpôt hāʾāreṣ wᵉyinnāʿᵃrû rᵉšāʿîm mimmennāh: 'So that it would seize the earth's edges and shake the wicked out of it.' The dawn's function: it grabs the edges of the earth (ʾāḥaz biḵanpôt hāʾāreṣ = grip the wings/corners of the earth) and shakes it like a garment being shaken to dislodge dirt (nāʿar = to shake out). The wicked (rᵉšāʿîm) who operate under cover of darkness are exposed and dislodged by the dawn — the daily sunrise is a daily moral event, a repeated act of divine justice that removes the dark's shelter from those who use it for evil (John 3:19-20; Eph 5:13).
14: titᵉhappēḵ kaḥōmer ḥôṯām wayyiṯyaṣᵉḇû kᵉmô lᵉḇûš: 'The earth changes like clay under a seal; its features stand out like a garment.' Kᵉḥōmer ḥôṯām (like clay under a seal) — a signet ring pressed into clay wax to make an impression; the dawn's light impresses features on the earth's surface just as a seal impresses a pattern in clay. Wayyiṯyaṣᵉḇû kᵉmô lᵉḇûš: 'they (the features) stand out like a garment' — the earth's topography is revealed by light, as features of fabric are revealed when light hits it. The dawn literally creates visible reality by revealing what darkness concealed.
15: wᵉyimmānaʿ mērᵉšāʿîm ʾôrām ûzᵉrôaʿ rāmāh tiššāḇēr: 'From the wicked their light is taken away, and the arm raised in violence shall be broken.' The dawn denies the wicked their darkness: yimmānaʿ mērᵉšāʿîm ʾôrām — their light is denied (paradoxically — the wicked's 'light' is the darkness in which they operate; the true light of day is their enemy). Zᵉrôaʿ rāmāh tiššāḇēr: 'the raised arm will be broken' — the arm of violent power, raised in the darkness, is broken by the morning. God's daily act of dawn is simultaneously the daily defeat of those who rely on darkness for their power.
16: hăḇāʾtā ʿal-niḇkê-yām ûḇᵉḥēqer tᵉhôm hiṯhallāḵtā: 'Have you gone down to the springs of the sea, or walked through the depths of the abyss?' Niḇkê yām (springs of the sea — where fresh springs emerge from the sea floor; known phenomena in the ancient world) and tᵉhôm (the deep, the abyss — the primordial deep of Gen 1:2). Job has not descended to the sea floor or walked through the primordial deep. The geography of the question is vertical: YHWH's governance extends to depths Job has never visited and cannot even conceive of fully.
17: hăniḡlû lᵉḵā šaʿărê māwet wᵉšaʿărê ṣalmāwet tirʾeh: 'Have the gates of death been revealed to you? Have you seen the doors of deepest darkness?' Šaʿărê māwet (gates of death) — the entrance to Sheol, the underworld (Ps 9:13; 107:18; Isa 38:10). Ṣalmāwet (deep shadow, death-shadow — possibly from ṣēl + māwet = shadow of death, though some parse it differently) — the darkness beyond death. Job has not passed through death's gates to verify their existence or structure. The question horizontally extends the previous vertical survey: above (storm, stars) and below (sea depths, death) — the full axis of created space is beyond Job's experience.
18: hiṯbônantā ʿaḏ-rōḥᵃḇê ʾāreṣ haggēḏ ʾim-yāḏaʿtā ḵullāh: 'Have you comprehended the earth's full breadth? Tell me, if you know all of it.' Rōḥab ʾāreṣ (breadth of the earth) — the horizontal extent. The survey is now complete: depth (v.16 sea, v.17 death), breadth (v.18 earth). YHWH's questions have mapped the created world's full spatial extent — and Job has visited none of its edges. The epistemological point: you cannot speak confidently about the governance of a creation whose dimensions you cannot grasp.
19: ʾê-zeh haddereḵ yiškon-ʾôr waḥōšeḵ ʾê-zeh mᵉqômô: 'Where is the way to where light lives? And where does darkness make its home?' Yiškon-ʾôr (where light dwells) — light is personified as a being with a dwelling place (šāḵan = to dwell, the word for the tabernacle's divine indwelling). The same for darkness: it has a mᵉqôm (place, habitation). The light and darkness that Genesis 1 describes as separated by God are here further personified as entities with cosmic addresses. Their addresses are known to God and unknown to Job.
20: kî tiqqāḥennû ʾel-gᵉḇûlô wᵉkî-ṯāḇîn nᵉṯîḇôṯ bêṯô: 'Can you take it to its territory and find the paths to its dwelling?' Light and darkness have their own territories (gᵉḇûl = border, boundary) and dwelling-paths. The ability to escort light or darkness to their proper domains would require knowledge of their cosmic geography — knowledge that only the original creator of the light-darkness division (Gen 1:3-5) possesses. The question is another form of 'were you there?' — present at the original ordering of light and darkness.
21: yāḏaʿtā kî-ʾāz tiwwālēḏ ûmispar yāmêḵā rabbîm: 'You know, of course — for you were born then, and the number of your days is very great!' The divine irony is devastating: kî-ʾāz tiwwālēḏ (for you were born back then!) — the sarcasm of impossible antiquity. Job was not born at creation; his days are not great enough to have witnessed the earth's founding. The irony inverts Job's complaint that his days are too short and his suffering too fresh: they are far too short to qualify him as a cosmological expert. The rhetorical irony here is the most artistically pointed moment in the divine speech.
22: hăḇāʾtā ʾel-ʾōṣᵉrôṯ šāleg wᵉʾōṣᵉrôṯ bārāḏ tirʾeh: 'Have you gone into the storehouses of the snow, or seen the storehouses of the hail?' ʾŌṣār (storehouse, treasury) — the weather phenomena are stored in divine treasuries, like a king's armory. Šāleg (snow) and bārāḏ (hail) are kept in reserve (ṣāpan = to store up, hide in reserve, v.23). The storehouse image implies intentionality: God is not the passive backdrop of weather systems but maintains a stockpile from which he deploys snow and hail for specific purposes.
23: ʾăšer-ḥāśaḵtî lᵉʿet-ṣār lᵉyôm qᵉrāb ûmilḥāmāh: 'Which I have kept in reserve for the time of trouble, for the day of battle and war.' The divine arsenal: snow and hail are not merely weather but weapons held in reserve for strategic moments. ʿEt ṣār (time of trouble/distress) and yôm qᵉrāb ûmilḥāmāh (day of battle and war) — God has cosmic weapons stockpiled for use in history's turning points. This was experienced literally in Israel's battles (Josh 10:11: hailstones against the Amorites; 1 Sam 7:10: thunder against the Philistines) and in eschatological judgment (Rev 8:7; 16:21).
24: ʾê-zeh haddereḵ yēḥāleq ʾôr yāp̄ēṣ qāḏîm ʿălê-ʾāreṣ: 'By what path does the light divide, or the east wind scatter itself across the earth?' The east wind (qāḏîm) — in the Levant the east wind (rûaḥ qādîm) was the hot, desiccating sirocco from the desert, capable of devastating crops and intensifying heat (Exod 14:21; Jonah 4:8; Hos 13:15). Its path is unknown to meteorologists and unknown to Job; only the one who sends it knows where it will go.
25: mî-p̄illag laššeṭep̄ tᵉʿālāh ûdereḵ ḥăzîz qōlôṯ: 'Who has cut a channel for the flood of waters, or a path for the thunderbolt?' Pālag (to divide, split, cut a channel) — irrigation canals are dug by human engineers; but who dug the channels that direct rainstorms? Ḥăzîz qōlôṯ (thunderbolt of the thunder-voice) — the lightning bolt also follows a path cut for it. The hydrological and electrical systems have their prescribed paths; God is the engineer who laid them out.
26: lᵉhaMṭîr ʿal-ʾereṣ lōʾ-ʾîš miḏḇār lōʾ-ʾāḏām bô: 'To bring rain on a land where no person lives, on the desert with no human in it.' God waters the wilderness where no human benefits from the rain. This single verse undoes utilitarian theodicy: not all divine action is organized around human benefit or human observation. Creation is not anthropocentric; God waters deserts for reasons that have nothing to do with human prosperity or suffering. The implication for Job's case: God's governance of the universe does not have human happiness as its organizing principle.
27: lᵉhaśbîaʿ šōʾāh ûmᵉšōʾāh ûlᵉhaṣmîaḥ môṣāʾ ḏeše: 'To satisfy the desolate wasteland and make tender grass spring up.' Šōʾāh ûmᵉšōʾāh (desolate wasteland) — the most barren landscape, where human life is impossible. God waters it to satisfy it (śābaʿ = to satisfy, fill to fullness) and make grass grow for no human observer. The satisfaction of the desert is an end in itself in the divine economy. Creation has intrinsic value before God independent of human use — a counter to the purely anthropocentric universe the friends assumed.
28: hăyēš-lāmāṭār ʾāḇ ʾô mî-hôlîḏ ʾeglê-ṭāl: 'Does the rain have a father? Who has fathered the drops of dew?' Rain and dew are personified as requiring a father (ʾāḇ = father, one who generates). Eglê ṭāl (drops of dew) — literally 'dewdrops.' The question of paternity: who generates rain and dew? Not human action; they arise from the divine-natural interaction that YHWH alone comprehends. The extended birth/parent metaphor (sea was born, v.8-9; now rain and dew need a father) presents the whole meteorological system as a family of phenomena fathered and mothered by God.
29: mibbeṭen mî yāṣāʾ haqqāraḥ ûkp̄ōr šāmayim mî yᵉlāḏô: 'From whose womb did the ice come? Who gave birth to the frost of heaven?' Qāraḥ (ice) and kp̄ôr (frost) are also personified as having a birth-womb. The birth-vocabulary for weather phenomena is theologically loaded: reḥem (womb) is the same word used for God's compassion (raḥămîm = compassion, from womb-roots). God is simultaneously the father and mother of the natural world's phenomena — the gendered metaphors are inclusive and complementary.
30: kāʾeḇen māyim yiṯḥabbeʾû ûp̄ᵉnê ṯᵉhôm yiṯlakkāḏû: 'The waters harden like stone, and the surface of the deep is locked.' The freezing of water: māyim... kāʾeḇen (waters like stone) — water solidifying to stone-hardness in winter. Ûp̄ᵉnê ṯᵉhôm yiṯlakkāḏû: 'the face of the deep is caught/locked' — the primordial deep (tᵉhôm of Gen 1:2) is locked in ice. The same chaos-waters that had to be bounded in creation (v.8-11) are seasonally locked by cold — a recurring divine act of ordering. Who commands this? Not Job.
31: hᵉṯaqqšēr maʿădannôṯ kîmāh ʾô-mošᵉkôṯ kᵉsîl tᵉpattēaḥ: 'Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades, or can you release the belt of Orion?' Kîmāh (Pleiades — the cluster of seven stars, visible spring through autumn) and Kᵉsîl (Orion — the winter constellation whose belt is three stars; also used metaphorically for the fool, kᵉsîl = fool). The chains (maʿădannôṯ = bonds, fetters) of the Pleiades and the belt (mošᵉkôṯ = bands, cords) of Orion are used as astronomical metaphors for constellation behavior. Can Job alter the seasonal positions of these stars? The question implies governance of the stellar calendar.
32: hătôṣîʾ mazzārôṯ bᵉʿittô wᵉʿayiš ʿal-bānêhā tanḥēm: 'Can you bring out Mazzaroth in its season? Can you guide the Bear with its cubs?' Mazzārôṯ is uncertain — possibly the zodiac, possibly the twelve constellations of the year, or possibly Venus as the morning star. ʿAyiš (the Bear — Ursa Major/the Big Dipper) and its cubs (bānêhā) — the circumpolar constellation that never sets in the northern sky. The question asks about seasonal guidance of these constellations. The astronomical vocabulary confirms the ancient world's detailed observation of stellar positions; YHWH is the guide of the celestial calendar.
33: hāyāḏaʿtā ḥuqqôṯ šāmayim ʾim-tāśîm mišṭārô bāʾāreṣ: 'Do you know the laws of heaven? Can you establish its rule over the earth?' Ḥuqqôṯ šāmayim (laws/statutes of heaven) — the fixed ordinances by which the heavens operate, the physical laws governing celestial bodies. Mišṭārô (its rule, dominion — from šāṭar = to write, rule) — can Job extend heaven's governance down to earth? The question is about the communication between celestial and terrestrial order. Job cannot write the laws of the stars, let alone enforce them on earth. The universe is a coherent rule-governed system whose constitution is divine.
34: hᵉṯārîm lāʿāḇ qôleḵā wᵉšip̄ʿat-māyim tᵉḵassêḵā: 'Can you raise your voice to the clouds and make a flood of water come down?' The question of meteorological command: human voice cannot address clouds with any effect. Tᵉḵassêḵā šip̄ʿat māyim (a flood of water covers you) — the answer to the divine cloud-command is the downpour that covers the earth. Job can call to the clouds; they will not answer. Only YHWH's voice commands the precipitation systems of the earth.
35: hᵉtᵉšallaḥ bᵉrāqîm wᵉyēlēḵû wᵉyōʾmᵉrû lᵉḵā hinnēnû: 'Can you send out lightning bolts on commission, and do they report back to you: “Here we are”?' The lightning as divine messengers: yᵉšallaḥ (to send, as a šālîaḥ = commissioned agent) — the lightning is dispatched like an ambassador and reports back (hinnēnû = here we are, the readiness formula used by Abraham at God's call, Gen 22:1, and Isaiah before God, Isa 6:8). The lightning obeys YHWH like a servant waiting to be sent; it does not obey Job. The image of lightning saying “here we are” is one of the divine speech's most vivid personifications.
36: mî-śāt baṭṭûḥôṯ ḥoḵmāh ʾô mî-nāṯan laśśeḵwî bînāh: 'Who has placed wisdom in the hidden self (ṭûḥôṯ), or given understanding to the mind (śeḵwî)?' Both ṭûḥôṯ and śeḵwî are hapaxes — their meanings are disputed. Ṭûḥôṯ: possibly 'inward parts, hidden self' (from Ugaritic cognate); śeḵwî: possibly 'cock' (rooster), 'mind,' or 'seeing-organ.' If referring to the rooster, the question is: who gave the rooster the instinct to announce the dawn? If referring to the human mind, the question is: who endowed humans with wisdom at all? Either reading points to the divine origin of natural intelligence and discernment.
37: mî-yᵉsappēr šᵉḥāqîm bᵉḥoḵmāh ûnᵉḇlê šāmayim mî yašᵉḵîḇ: 'Who has the wisdom to count the clouds? Who can tilt the water jars of heaven?' Sāpar šᵉḥāqîm (count the clouds) — a task no human can accomplish, either because of sheer number or because of their constant change. Nᵉḇlê šāmayim (water jars of heaven) — the clouds as jars (nēḇel = skin-jar, jug) full of water that God tilts to pour. The tilting metaphor is deliberately domestic and intimate: the master of the universe manages weather with the same intentionality as a servant serving water from a jar. Divine sovereignty operates at all scales simultaneously.
38: bᵉṣeqet ʿāp̄ār lammuṣṣāq ûrᵉḡāḇîm yiḏḇāqû: 'When the dust hardens into a mass and the clods of dirt cling together?' The condition that follows the tilting of the water jars (v.37): when rain falls, dust hardens to mud and clods cling. The transformation of loose dry soil into cohesive wet earth is another ordinary observation presented as a divine act: God manages the water cycle down to its soil-level effects. The same God who counts clouds also knows what happens to the dirt when rain hits it.
39: hᵉṯaṣṣûḏ laʾaryēh ṭārep̄ wᵉḥayyat kᵉp̄îrîm tᵉmallēʾ: 'Do you hunt prey for the lion and fill the hunger of its cubs?' The divine speech shifts from cosmic (stars, seas, weather) to biological (animals). God is the hunter-provider for lions — not a distant deity but an active sustainer of the carnivores. Ṭārep̄ (prey, torn food) — God hunts prey for animals. This is the theodicy answer in miniature: God provides for the predators too, not just the meek. The universe's food chain is sustained by divine provision at every level.
40: kî yišḥû bammᵉʿônôṯ yēšᵉḇû bassukāh lᵉʾāraḇ: 'When they crouch in their dens and wait in ambush in their thickets?' The lion's hunting posture: šāḥāh (to bow down, crouch) in its den (mᵉʿônāh = den, lair, dwelling of wild animals — used metaphorically for God's dwelling in Ps 26:8; 68:6). God knows the posture of each lion as it crouches in ambush. The divine knowledge extends from the stellar (v.31-33) to the predatory animal's daily hunting position. Omniscience of the particular and the cosmic simultaneously.
41: mî yāḵîn lāʿōrēḇ ṣêḏô kî-yaldāyw ʾel-ʾēl yᵉšawwēʿû yiṯʿū bᵉlî-ʾōḵel: 'Who provides the raven its food when its chicks cry out to God and wander about hungry?' The raven (ʿōrēḇ) — the bird associated with wilderness and death in the OT (1 Kgs 17:4-6: ravens fed Elijah; Gen 8:7: the raven from the ark). Its chicks cry to God (ʾel-ʾēl yᵉšawwēʿû) when hungry — the raven's young are presented as petitioners before God. God hears and provides for the ravens, whose young cry out. The divine speech ends its first movement with a crying creature whose petition God meets — an implicit question to Job: if God hears the ravens, will he not hear you?
